A lot more details in the description on how you can apply for this subsidy share it with someone who may need it.

caption:
Among subsidies being offered by the Govt, this one is quite interesting...

35% of the Project Cost is being offered by the Govt through the National Horticulture Borad (NHB) as well as the Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI) for you to set up a...

Cold Chain Warehouse.

The need is rapidly increasing around the world for not just the fact that people want more exotic agri produce form around the world like Avocados, Blueberries, Monk Fruit, Edamame all through the year...

But also pharma products like vaccines and Ozempic.

The current cold chain warehousing facilities are not even sufficient to meet the booming demand.

Based on my conversations, half the people say their warehouses are underutilized (possible poor selection of location), and the other half feel it’s so undercapacity that they wish they had the money to open more.

Don’t just be prudent about the location, but also about the uninterrupted power supply in that area... It’s the second biggest determinant for this model.

The market is said to be growing at a CAGR of 10% globally, and in India and Africa, it should be upwards of 12% p.a. for the next 5 years.

The NHB has a subsidy of 35% of capital investment for construction, renovation and expansion of 5000 MT to 10000 MT of capacity.

The MoFPI has the Kisan Sampada Yojana to promote the same.

#casarthakahuja

transcript: There are five documents and agreements that every business owner must have, but more than 90% of entrepreneurs in India don't even have one out of these five. And I'll explain. The first one is a co-founders agreement or a shareholders agreement. And the biggest mistake that people make is that they use the template articles of association while registering their company that their CA will copy-paste on every company that they register. Now, what these articles of association does not cover. Tomorrow, imagine your business partner stops contributing in the business or leaves the business but has 50% shareholding. What are you going to do? You will continue to work while they will reap the benefits. You can't shut down the business. You can't sell the business, which is why you need two add clauses which specify what if any of the two partners stops contributing, then how will the other partner buy their shares at what price? What is going to be the dispute resolution mechanism? All of this needs to be put in. The second kind of agreement is IP assignment agreement because a lot of times people end up buying domain names, trade names in their individual names and later register a company and it is never transferred to the company now imagine your business partner registered the domain name in their personal name but there was never any IP assignment agreement tomorrow if they leave your brand has practically no value you will not even be able to sell it in the market Then comes the third agreement, which is the privacy policy that you've never read on your website. Now, a number of countries, for example in Europe, there are GDPR regulations. In India now, there is a DDPD Act, which means it becomes mandatory for companies to disclose who is using the data which is collected on the website, where is it stored, what all parties do you share it with. It has become a legal requirement. Then comes the fourth thing, which is the terms of service. Again, published on your website, which you've never read. It becomes really important if you are a B2C business or a B2B business catering to international clients because you need to specify what is your product or service, what does it cover, what does it not cover, what kind of liabilities will you not take ownership for, and what is going to be the jurisdiction in case of a dispute. Then comes the fifth agreement, which is employment agreement or vendor agreements that have these specific clauses that mention that tomorrow if the employee leaves or the vendor leaves whatever work they have done, that IP rests with the company and they are bound by confidentiality. They cannot start sharing that information with anyone else. I've given a lot more in the description. Share it with someone who may need it.

caption:
Every Founder must have these 5 Documents for their Business... and most founders don’t have even 1...

👉🏻 1/ Co-Founder’s Agreement - biggest reason for failure of business... so include the following...

a) What will happen to the shares if one of the founders leaves the company in between (Reverse Vesting)
b) Decision making power function-wise split between the founders for clarity
c) Dispute Resolution mechanism on items that they can’t agree on

A Co-Founder’s Agreement should also become part of the Articles of Association after execution, and filed with the MCA, as most companies just have the template AOA prescribed by the MCA and don’t care to amend it to suit their needs.

👉🏻 2/ IP Assignment Agreement...

As a number of times, either of the founders end up buying things like domain name, brand name registered in their own name, even before the company is incorporated, and these IP assets are never passed on to the company.

Thus, if a founder leaves with the IP, the company’s brand is locked in a dispute, and the remaining founder cannot even sell it.

👉🏻 3/ Terms of Service listed on the website, which should include...

a) What your product/service does (and critically, what it doesn’t do)
b) User obligations and prohibited uses
c) Limitation of liability clauses
d) Refund and cancellation policies
e) Dispute resolution and jurisdiction clauses

👉🏻 4/ Privacy Policy

It’s now legally mandated in a number of countries (India’s DPDP Act, EU’s GDPR, etc.). Your Privacy Policy must outline...

a) What data you collect and why
b) How you store and protect this data
c) Who you share it with (third-party tools, payment processors)
d) User rights regarding data (access, deletion, portability)
e) Cookie usage and tracking

👉🏻 5/ Employment Agreement or Vendor Agreement...

a) Clear IP assignment clause
b) Non-compete and non-solicitation clauses
c) Confidentiality obligations that survive beyond the engagement

#casarthakahuja

caption:
Thinking of starting your own business but short on capital?

The government’s Pradhan Mantri Mudra Yojana is not just a policy on paper, it’s one of India’s most widely used small-business credit programmes.

Under Mudra:
	•	Loans are collateral-free
	•	Businesses across services, trading, manufacturing and small enterprises are eligible
	•	Entrepreneurs can access funding through banks, NBFCs, MFIs and small finance banks

So far:
	•	50+ crore loan accounts have been sanctioned
	•	₹30+ lakh crore has been disbursed nationwide
	•	States like Tamil Nadu, Uttar Pradesh, Karnataka and Maharashtra have seen the highest uptake, showing real on-ground adoption

For growing businesses, borrowers with a strong repayment track record can now move beyond the traditional ₹10 lakh Mudra limit through extended credit options supported by the government’s MSME framework.

Applications can be made:
	•	Directly at your bank branch, or
	•	Online via the JanSamarth portal

If lack of capital has been the only thing holding someone back from starting a business, this is worth knowing and worth sharing.

#MSME #MudraYojana #Entrepreneurship #SmallBusiness #FinancialInclusion StartupIndia
